title: Path Hop Count Analysis
dataSources: `index=thousandeyes_pathvis sourcetype=cisco:thousandeyes:path-vis` from the path visualization collection in `ta_cisco_thousandeyes` (Splunkbase 7719). Path Visualization data is collected via the ThousandEyes REST API (not the OTel stream) at a configurable polling interval (default: 300 seconds). When you enable the Tests Stream — Metrics input, the app can optionally enable path visualization collection into a separate index. Key fields: `hop_count` (integer — total hops from agent to target), `thousandeyes.source.agent.name`, `server.address`, `thousandeyes.test.type`, `thousandeyes.permalink`. Volume: ~5 KB per agent-target pair per poll ≈ 1.4 MB/agent-target/day at 5-minute intervals.
app: `ta_cisco_thousandeyes` — Cisco ThousandEyes App for Splunk ([Splunkbase 7719](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/7719)), Cisco Supported.
spl:
`path_viz_index` thousandeyes.test.type="agent-to-server"
| stats min(hop_count) as min_hops max(hop_count) as max_hops by thousandeyes.source.agent.name, server.address
| where max_hops - min_hops > 2
| sort -max_hops
description: Detects Agent-to-Server paths where the hop count varied by more than 2 hops within the search window, indicating routing instability, BGP path changes, or load-balancer-induced path variation. A sudden increase in hop count often correlates with higher latency because each additional hop adds processing and propagation delay.
value: When a carrier reroutes traffic — whether due to a fiber cut, maintenance, or BGP policy change — the new path often traverses more hops through different cities or providers. Each extra hop adds 1–5 ms of latency and introduces another potential failure point. Catching hop count changes in Splunk gives the WAN team early warning that a path has shifted before the latency increase triggers SLA violations, and provides the evidence (which hops changed, when) needed to open a carrier ticket or adjust SD-WAN policy.
implementation: Enable path visualization data collection in the Tests Stream — Metrics input by checking "Include Network Path Data" (or use the `--pathvis` flag during setup). Configure the `path_viz_index` macro to point to the `thousandeyes_pathvis` index. Schedule the search every 30 minutes over the last 4 hours to capture gradual path changes.
visualization: (1) Table: agent, server, min hops, max hops, hop delta — sorted by largest delta so paths with the most instability surface first. (2) Timechart: `| timechart span=15m avg(hop_count) by thousandeyes.source.agent.name` to spot step-changes in hop count over time. (3) Single value: count of paths with hop delta > 2 (yellow threshold ≥ 1). (4) Drilldown to ThousandEyes Path Visualization via `thousandeyes.permalink` to see the actual hop-by-hop topology.

references.title: ThousandEyes Splunk App — Inputs (Path Visualization collection)
ThousandEyes Path Visualization — Understanding network paths

knownFalsePositives:
**ECMP load balancing.** When traffic crosses a network with Equal-Cost Multi-Path routing (common in data centers and large ISP backbones), consecutive test probes may take different paths with different hop counts. The hop count fluctuates by 1–2 hops continuously — this is normal ECMP behavior, not instability. Distinguish by checking whether the variation is consistent and small (±1–2 hops) over days without any correlated latency impact. Raise the threshold to `> 3` for paths known to traverse ECMP networks.

**Carrier maintenance windows.** Carriers routinely reroute traffic during maintenance, temporarily increasing hop counts. This typically happens during off-peak hours (02:00–06:00 local) and lasts 2–4 hours. Distinguish by checking whether the hop count change is temporary and reverts to baseline after the window. Suppress with a `carrier_maintenance_windows` lookup.

**Anycast destination changes.** If the target server uses anycast (common for CDNs, DNS resolvers like 8.8.8.8, and cloud load balancers), the test may reach different physical servers at different times, each with different hop counts. This is normal anycast behavior. Distinguish by checking whether `server.address` resolves to different IPs across rounds (anycast) or the same IP (routing change).
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• All prerequisites from UC-5.9.1 apply (app installed, OAuth authenticated, HEC configured).
• **Path Visualization data collection must be enabled.** This is a separate data flow from the OTel metrics stream — it uses the ThousandEyes REST API (polling), not HEC push. When you create the Tests Stream — Metrics input, check **"Include Network Path Data"** to enable path visualization collection. Alternatively, if the setup script was used: `setup.sh --enable-inputs ... --pathvis` enables it. Use `--no-pathvis` to disable if you only want metrics.
• **Separate index:** Path visualization data goes to `thousandeyes_pathvis` (sourcetype `cisco:thousandeyes:path-vis`), not `thousandeyes_metrics`. The `path_viz_index` macro must be configured to point to this index: **Settings → Advanced Search → Search Macros → path_viz_index** should expand to `index=thousandeyes_pathvis`.
• **Polling interval:** Path visualization data is collected at a configurable interval (default: 300 seconds / 5 minutes). Unlike the metrics stream (60-second rounds), path data is more expensive to collect and process, so a longer interval is appropriate.
• **API rate limits:** Path Visualization API calls count against ThousandEyes API rate limits (organization-level). If you have many tests, the polling interval may need to be increased to avoid HTTP 429 throttling. Check the app logs for throttling warnings.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
If path visualization was not enabled during initial setup:
1. Navigate to **Inputs → Tests Stream — Metrics → Edit** (or create a new input if none exists).
2. Check **"Include Network Path Data"**.
3. Set the Path Visualization index to `thousandeyes_pathvis`.
4. Set the polling interval (default 300 seconds is appropriate for most deployments; increase to 600 for large test counts).
5. Save. The app begins polling the ThousandEyes Path Visualization API.

Verification:
```spl
index=thousandeyes_pathvis sourcetype="cisco:thousandeyes:path-vis" earliest=-30m
| stats count by thousandeyes.source.agent.name, server.address
```
You should see rows for each agent-target pair with active Agent-to-Server tests. If no data, check:
- The `path_viz_index` macro definition
- App logs: `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ExecProcessor "thousandeyes" "path"` for API errors
- ThousandEyes API rate limiting: look for HTTP 429 in the logs

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
```spl
`path_viz_index` thousandeyes.test.type="agent-to-server"
| stats min(hop_count) as min_hops max(hop_count) as max_hops by thousandeyes.source.agent.name, server.address
| where max_hops - min_hops > 2
| sort -max_hops
```

**Understanding this SPL**

`path_viz_index` — expands to `index=thousandeyes_pathvis` (the app-defined macro for path visualization data, separate from the `stream_index` macro used for OTel metrics).

`stats min(hop_count) ... max(hop_count)` — captures the range of hop counts observed for each agent-target pair over the search window. A stable path produces `min_hops = max_hops` (delta = 0). A delta of 1–2 is typically ECMP or minor carrier adjustments. A delta > 2 suggests a significant routing change.

`where max_hops - min_hops > 2` — the threshold of 2 is a practical balance: strict enough to catch meaningful routing changes, loose enough to avoid ECMP noise. For critical paths where any routing change matters (e.g., trading floor to exchange), tighten to `> 0` or `> 1`. For paths traversing large ISP backbones with known ECMP, relax to `> 3` or `> 4`.

**Enhanced variant** — to detect hop count changes that ALSO correlate with latency degradation:
```spl
`path_viz_index` thousandeyes.test.type="agent-to-server"
| stats min(hop_count) as min_hops max(hop_count) as max_hops latest(hop_count) as current_hops by thousandeyes.source.agent.name, server.address
| where max_hops - min_hops > 2
| join type=left thousandeyes.source.agent.name, server.address [
  search `stream_index` thousandeyes.test.type="agent-to-server"
  | stats avg(network.latency) as avg_lat_s by thousandeyes.source.agent.name, server.address
  | eval avg_lat_ms=round(avg_lat_s*1000,1)
]
| eval impact=if(avg_lat_ms>100, "Latency impacted", "No latency impact")
| sort -max_hops
```
This join correlates hop count changes with latency metrics from the streaming data, letting you prioritize paths where the routing change actually degraded performance.

**Scheduling:** cron `*/30 * * * *`, time range `-4h to now` (longer window to capture gradual path changes). Trigger on "Number of results > 0". Throttle by `thousandeyes.source.agent.name` + `server.address` for 8 hours.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) **Cross-reference ThousandEyes UI.** Navigate to **Cloud & Enterprise Agents → Views → Path Visualization** for a specific test. The visual path topology shows each hop as a node. Count the hops manually in the UI and compare to the `hop_count` field in Splunk.

(b) **Stable path baseline.** For a path you know is stable (e.g., same-datacenter or dedicated circuit), `min_hops` should equal `max_hops`. If they differ on a stable path, the `hop_count` field extraction may be incorrect — check the raw events.

(c) **Verify field name.** The `hop_count` field name may vary depending on the app version and how path visualization data is structured. Check with: `index=thousandeyes_pathvis earliest=-30m | fieldsummary | sort -count | head 30` to identify the correct field name for hop count in your deployment.

Step 4 — Operationalize
**Dashboard** (add as a panel in UC-5.9.33 "Cloud Provider Path Visualization" dashboard or standalone "Path Stability"):
• Table: agent | target | min hops | max hops | delta | current hops | latency impact — sorted by delta descending.
• Timechart: hop count over 24 hours per agent-target pair. Step-changes in the chart indicate routing events.

**Runbook** (owner: Network Operations / WAN team):
1. When hop count increases significantly (delta > 3), open ThousandEyes Path Visualization and compare the current path to a previous stable path.
2. Identify which hops are new (appeared after the change) and which disappeared.
3. If new hops are in a different city or ISP, a BGP route change occurred. Check UC-5.9.9 (BGP Path Change Trending) and UC-5.9.10 (BGP Update Volume) for correlated BGP events.
4. If hop count increased AND latency increased proportionally (each hop adds ~2–5 ms), open a ticket with the ISP asking about the route change.
5. If hop count increased but latency is stable or improved, the new path may be intentional (carrier optimization) — monitor but don't escalate.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **No data in `thousandeyes_pathvis`** — Path visualization collection is not enabled. Check the Tests Stream — Metrics input configuration for "Include Network Path Data". Also verify the `path_viz_index` macro points to the correct index.

• **`hop_count` field is always null** — The field name may differ in your app version. Run `| fieldsummary` on the raw path visualization data to find the correct field name for hop count.

• **Path data arrives sporadically** — The API polling interval may be too short, causing ThousandEyes API rate limiting (HTTP 429). Increase the polling interval to 600 seconds or higher.

• **All common troubleshooting** — See UC-5.9.1 Step 5 for OAuth, HEC, and general app troubleshooting.
grandmaExplanation: We count how many stops our traffic makes on the way to its destination, because when the number suddenly changes it means the internet took a different road — and that new road might be slower.
